nativeRange.habitat: Caribbean and tropical western Atlantic coral reefs; the scallop wedges itself into shaded rock crevices, overhangs, and self-built rubble 'nests' anchored by byssal threads, often filter-feeding on plankton drifting past the reef edge.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with established biofilm
rubble or coral fragments the scallop can wedge into
low ambient light at the chosen perch

diet.notes: The Flame Scallop is an obligate filter feeder that derives no nutrition from light and cannot photosynthesize. It must be fed live or refrigerated phytoplankton — genera such as Tetraselmis, Tisochrysis, Nannochloropsis, and Rhodomonas are commonly used — along with very small zooplankton such as copepod nauplii and rotifers, dosed multiple times daily or ideally on a slow near-continuous drip. Phytoplankton particles in the roughly 5-to-40-micron range are preferred, while zooplankton particles should generally be under around 200 microns. Sustained, adequate feeding is the single most important factor in long-term survival. In standard reef systems the water column is typically too nutrient-lean to support these animals, and most slowly starve over a period of weeks to months even when outwardly they appear healthy.

goodWith: small peaceful reef fish (clownfish, gobies, blennies)
other filter-feeding inverts in heavily-fed tanks
avoidWith: aggressive corals with long sweeper tentacles

imageCandidates.notes: Matthew Hoelscher; 1920x2560. Sharp in-situ shot of a live open Ctenoides scaber (as synonym Lima scabra) wedged in a reef crevice, showing the full brilliant red mantle and complete ring of rippling white-tipped tentacles along both valve edges - the diagnostic flame-scallop look.
Dan Schofield; 2048x1536. Crisp side profile showing the textured file-clam shell plus the bright red mantle and pale tentacle fringe; good complementary angle documenting the bivalve shell.
Pauline Walsh Jacobson; 960x720. Vivid, sharp close-up of the electric red-orange mantle and dense rippling tentacles; carries a small corner signature watermark, hence non-recommended.

summary: The Flame Scallop is a striking Caribbean filter-feeding bivalve, known for its brilliant red mantle and rippling tentacles. It is notoriously difficult to sustain in standard reef tanks and is generally considered a specialist-only animal that requires consistent phytoplankton dosing to survive long-term.
careNotes: The Flame Scallop is a Caribbean reef-dwelling file clam (family Limidae) whose bright red mantle and rippling tentacles make it one of the most visually arresting bivalves in the trade. The flashing red 'electric' appearance often credited to bioluminescence is actually a combination of dense carotenoid pigmentation in the mantle plus light reflection from highly reflective tissue along the mantle edge — there is no light production. Adults reach roughly 7-10 cm and are found across the wider Caribbean from Florida and the Bahamas south through the Antilles and along the northern coasts of South and Central America, typically 1-40 m down on coral reefs, where they wedge into shaded crevices or build rubble nests anchored by byssal threads. They are obligate non-photosynthetic filter feeders. In a standard reef system the dissolved plankton load is far too low to sustain them, so most specimens slowly starve over a span of weeks to a few months even though the shell stays closed and the mantle remains visible — by the time the animal visibly thins, recovery is rare. Generally advised to keep only in mature systems with a productive refugium and an active phytoplankton dosing regime (live or refrigerated phyto plus copepod nauplii / rotifers, dosed multiple times daily or via a slow drip). Water parameters track standard reef values: temperature around 23-26 C, pH 8.1-8.4, specific gravity 1.023-1.026, kH 8-12 dKH, stable Ca/Mg as for any reef invertebrate. They prefer shaded, low-to-moderate-flow placements in rockwork rather than open sandbed; they are mobile and may shift position by 'jumping' with their valves if conditions are not to their liking. Like all marine molluscs they are copper-sensitive and should never be exposed to copper-based medications. Tankmates should be small, peaceful, and non-predatory — large angels, triggers, puffers, predatory wrasses, hermit crabs, and stinging anemones have all been reported to pick at or consume the mantle and tentacles.
breedingNotes: Not bred in captivity. In the wild Ctenoides scaber is a broadcast spawner that releases gametes into the water column where fertilization produces planktonic veliger larvae; the rearing pathway has not been worked out in the aquarium hobby, and trade animals are wild-collected.

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 404 for the species page on access; swapped in TopShelf Aquatics and Reeflings as alternative secondaries per playbook fallback rule.

additional.name: TopShelf Aquatics - Flame Scallop
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (3-4 in / ~7.6-10 cm), temperature (74-78 F = 23-26 C), pH (8.1-8.4), specific gravity (1.024-1.026), 30+ gal minimum tank, obligate filter feeding on phytoplankton (Tetraselmis, Tisochrysis, Rhodomonas), peaceful temperament, reef-safe, lifespan 1-3 years.

additional.name: Reeflings - Ctenoides scaber Care Guide
additional.notes: Third confirming source for adult size (10 cm), temperature 22-26 C, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.026, moderate-to-difficult care level, peaceful temperament; also surfaced synonym list (Ctenoides scabra, Lima aspera, Lima glacialis, Lima scabra, Ostrea spp.) and starvation as primary cause of captive death.

additional.name: Wikipedia - Ctenoides scaber
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Limidae / Limida), Caribbean distribution, confirmation that Lima scabra is a junior synonym, and that the red mantle color is carotenoid pigmentation rather than bioluminescence.

additional.name: ReefGuide - Rough Fileclam (Ctenoides scaber)
additional.notes: Wild-occurrence depth range 1-40 m (3-130 ft) across Caribbean, Bahamas, and Florida; habitat use in shaded crevices and self-built rubble nests.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Near-universal slow starvation in standard reef systems; requires sustained phytoplankton dosing and mature live-rock biofilm to survive long-term.
